---
title: "PSC Secretary — System Reference Manual"
subtitle: "Submissions & Commission Decision Management System"
author: "Office of the Public Service Commission, Vanuatu"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
lang: en

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    smooth-scroll: true
    embed-resources: true
    page-layout: article
    grid:
      sidebar-width: 260px
      body-width: 840px
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
   Secretary's Reference Manual — Design
   Deliberately minimal; document-first aesthetic
   ───────────────────────────────────────────────── */

:root {
  --ink:      #1c2536;
  --ink-mid:  #4a5568;
  --ink-soft: #718096;
  --rule:     #cbd5e0;
  --accent:   #1d4ed8;
  --bg:       #ffffff;
}

body {
  font-family: "Georgia", "Times New Roman", serif;
  font-size: 1rem;
  line-height: 1.85;
  color: var(--ink);
  background: var(--bg);
}

/* ── Title block ── */
#title-block-header {
  padding: 2.5rem 0 1.5rem;
  border-bottom: 3px double var(--ink);
  margin-bottom: 2.5rem;
}
#title-block-header .title {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: 2rem;
  font-weight: 800;
  letter-spacing: -.03em;
  color: var(--ink);
  margin-bottom: .2rem;
}
#title-block-header .subtitle {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .95rem;
  color: var(--ink-mid);
  font-style: normal;
  margin-bottom: .75rem;
}
#title-block-header .quarto-title-meta {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .82rem;
  color: var(--ink-soft);
}

/* ── Headings ── */
h1 {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: 1.65rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: var(--ink);
  border-bottom: 2px solid var(--accent);
  padding-bottom: .35rem;
  margin-top: 3rem;
  letter-spacing: -.02em;
}
h2 {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: 1.2rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: var(--ink);
  border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);
  padding-bottom: .2rem;
  margin-top: 2rem;
}
h3 {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: 1rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: var(--ink-mid);
  margin-top: 1.6rem;
}
h4 {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .93rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: var(--ink-mid);
  margin-top: 1.2rem;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  letter-spacing: .05em;
}
p { margin-bottom: .9rem; }
strong { color: var(--ink); }

/* ── TOC ── */
#TOC {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .82rem;
  line-height: 1.65;
}
#TOC a { color: var(--ink-mid); text-decoration: none; }
#TOC a:hover { color: var(--accent); }
#TOC .active { color: var(--accent); font-weight: 600; }

/* ── Numbered procedure lists ── */
ol.procedure {
  list-style: none;
  counter-reset: proc;
  padding: 0;
  margin: 1.1rem 0 1.4rem;
}
ol.procedure > li {
  counter-increment: proc;
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1.8rem 1fr;
  column-gap: .85rem;
  padding: .55rem 0;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #f1f5f9;
  font-size: .96rem;
}
ol.procedure > li:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
ol.procedure > li::before {
  content: counter(proc) ".";
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: .82rem;
  color: var(--accent);
  padding-top: .18rem;
  text-align: right;
}

/* ── Call-out boxes ── */
.note, .warn, .crit, .tip {
  padding: .7rem 1rem;
  border-left: 3px solid;
  border-radius: 0 .35rem .35rem 0;
  margin: 1.25rem 0;
  font-size: .93rem;
}
.box-head {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .7rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  letter-spacing: .1em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  margin-bottom: .2rem;
}
.note  { background: #eff6ff; border-left-color: #3b82f6; }
.note  .box-head { color: #1d4ed8; }
.warn  { background: #fffbeb; border-left-color: #d97706; }
.warn  .box-head { color: #92400e; }
.crit  { background: #fff1f2; border-left-color: #dc2626; }
.crit  .box-head { color: #9f1239; }
.tip   { background: #f0fdf4; border-left-color: #16a34a; }
.tip   .box-head { color: #14532d; }

/* Quarto built-in callout overrides */
.callout { border-radius: 0 .35rem .35rem 0; border-left-width: 3px; margin: 1.25rem 0; }

/* ── Chapter section dividers ── */
.section-break {
  display: flex;
  align-items: baseline;
  gap: 1.25rem;
  margin: 3.5rem 0 1.75rem;
  padding-bottom: .6rem;
  border-bottom: 2px solid var(--ink);
}
.section-num {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .7rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  letter-spacing: .14em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  color: var(--accent);
  white-space: nowrap;
}
.section-title {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: 1.3rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: var(--ink);
}

/* ── Workflow chain ── */
.workflow {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .85rem;
  color: var(--ink-mid);
  padding: .6rem .8rem;
  border: 1px solid var(--rule);
  border-radius: .3rem;
  background: #f8fafc;
  margin: 1rem 0;
  letter-spacing: .01em;
}

/* ── Tables ── */
table {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: .86rem;
  width: 100%;
  border-collapse: collapse;
  margin: 1.1rem 0 1.5rem;
}
thead tr { background: var(--ink); color: #fff; }
thead th {
  padding: .48rem .75rem;
  text-align: left;
  font-weight: 600;
  font-size: .8rem;
  letter-spacing: .04em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
}
tbody td {
  padding: .44rem .75rem;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #e2e8f0;
  vertical-align: top;
}
tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f8fafc; }
tbody tr:hover { background: #eff6ff; }

/* ── Pre-meeting checklist ── */
ul.checklist {
  list-style: none;
  padding: 0;
  margin: 1rem 0 1.5rem;
}
ul.checklist li {
  display: flex;
  align-items: flex-start;
  gap: .7rem;
  padding: .38rem 0;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #f1f5f9;
  font-size: .94rem;
}
ul.checklist li:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
ul.checklist li::before {
  content: "☐";
  font-size: 1rem;
  color: #94a3b8;
  flex-shrink: 0;
  margin-top: .05rem;
}

/* ── Stage reference table ── */
.stage-name {
  font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: var(--ink);
  font-size: .84rem;
}
.stage-code {
  font-family: monospace;
  font-size: .78rem;
  color: var(--ink-soft);
}

/* ── Keyboard key display ── */
kbd {
  font-family: monospace;
  font-size: .82rem;
  padding: .1rem .4rem;
  border: 1px solid #d1d5db;
  border-radius: .25rem;
  background: #f9fafb;
  color: var(--ink);
  box-shadow: 0 1px 0 #d1d5db;
}

/* ── Print ── */
@media print {
  #TOC, .quarto-sidebar { display: none !important; }
  body { font-size: .9rem; line-height: 1.65; }
  h1 { page-break-before: always; }
  ol.procedure > li, .callout { break-inside: avoid; }
}
</style>
```

**Audience:** PSC Secretary and Senior Administration Officer

This manual covers the complete SCDMS workflow from the Secretary's perspective — receiving ministry submissions through to formal decision records and implementation follow-up. It assumes you are already signed in and familiar with navigating the application. For sign-in and two-factor authentication, see the main SCDMS User Guide.

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Section 1</span><span class="section-title">The Register — Receiving and Processing Submissions</span></div>
```

# The Submission Register

The submission register is the central record of every case submitted to the PSC. As Secretary, you have full visibility over all submissions regardless of stage or unit assignment, and you are responsible for the formal intake steps that transform a ministry-submitted case into an active PSC matter.

## When a Submission Arrives

Ministries submit through SCDMS directly. You receive an automatic system notification the moment a submission reaches **Submitted to PSC** status. The notification appears in the bell icon in the header and is also sent to your registered email address.

Do not action a submission immediately on arrival. Allow 24 hours for any final documents to be added, then begin the intake process.

::: {.callout-note}
High volumes before a Commission sitting are normal. Use the **Submissions** list with the status filter set to *Submitted to PSC* to work through the intake queue in order of submission date.
:::

## Acknowledging Receipt

Open the submission from the queue. The first intake action is to formally acknowledge receipt, which:

- Changes the stage from *Submitted to PSC* to *Received by PSC*
- Timestamps the receipt in the stage history
- Notifies the submitting ministry that their case has been received

Click the **Acknowledge Receipt** button in the Workflow Controls panel on the right side of the submission detail page. A confirmation dialog appears — confirm to proceed. No additional fields are required at this stage.

## Registering a Submission

Registration is the formal assignment of a PSC reference number and the routing of the case to the appropriate processing unit.

<ol class="procedure">
<li>From the *Received* stage, click **Register & Route** in the Workflow Controls panel.</li>
<li>The registration dialog presents the case title, form type, and ministry for confirmation.</li>
<li>Enter the **PSC Reference Number** in the format *PSC-YYYY-NNNNN* (e.g. *PSC-2025-00341*). This is the number that will appear in all subsequent correspondence.</li>
<li>Select the **Processing Unit** — HR Unit, VIPAM, ODU, or other — based on the nature of the case and the form type. The system shows the current queue depth for each unit to assist allocation decisions.</li>
<li>Optionally assign a specific **Unit Manager** within that unit. If left blank, the submission appears in the unit's general queue.</li>
<li>Click **Register**. The stage advances to *Registered and Routed* and the assigned unit manager is notified.</li>
</ol>

Once registered, the submission is no longer in your active queue. Your next involvement will be when the unit manager advances it to the Commission stage, or if you need to intervene (see below).

## Returns, Withdrawals, and Corrections

**If a ministry needs to correct a submitted case**, they contact you directly. The unit manager (or you, if it has not yet been assigned) uses the **Return to Ministry** action in the Workflow Controls panel. Specify the reason clearly — the ministry receives an automated notification containing your text verbatim. When the ministry re-submits, the case returns to your intake queue.

**Withdrawal** is an irreversible action. The ministry must request withdrawal in writing. Once you apply it in the system (using the Withdraw action in Workflow Controls), the submission is closed and cannot be re-opened. Keep the written request on file.

**Duplicate submissions** occasionally occur — for instance, a ministry submits the same matter twice. Identify duplicates early: the submissions list shows form type and officer name, making duplicates easy to spot. Contact the ministry to confirm which submission to retain, withdraw the duplicate, and note the reason in the submission's notes field.

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Section 2</span><span class="section-title">Commission Sittings</span></div>
```

# Preparing for a Commission Sitting

The Secretary carries full responsibility for Commission sitting management — from creating the meeting record to issuing post-sitting decisions. This section covers preparation. Running the meeting itself is covered in Section 3.

## Creating a Meeting Record

Navigate to **Commission Decision → Meetings** in the left sidebar. Click **Schedule Meeting** (top right).

Fill in the meeting details:

| Field | Notes |
|---|---|
| Meeting date | Date of the sitting |
| Meeting type | Regular, Special, or Emergency |
| Location / platform | Physical room or virtual (include link if hybrid) |
| Quorum | Minimum number of Commissioners required |
| Notes | Any administrative notes for internal reference |

Save the meeting record. It is now visible to all Commissioners and PSC officers with secretariat access, though its agenda is still empty.

## Building the Agenda

Open the meeting from the Meetings list, then click the **Agenda** tab. The agenda is built by adding submissions that are ready for Commission consideration — submissions must be in the *Forwarded to Commission* stage to be eligible.

**Adding items:**

Click **Add Item** and search for the submission by reference number or title. The system filters for eligible submissions automatically. Select the submission; a new agenda item is created.

For each item, set:
- **Category** — Appointments, Promotions, Discipline, or other categories your sitting uses. Items are grouped by category in the printed agenda and briefing pack.
- **Sequence** — The order within the category. Drag items to reorder, or edit the sequence number directly.
- **Matters Arising reference** — If the item is a matter arising from a previous sitting, link it to the originating meeting. This maintains the formal record chain.

**Ordering guidance:** Convention at the PSC places routine matters (confirmations, transfers) early in the sitting, more complex matters (discipline, appointments at senior grades) later. Matters arising from previous sittings are typically handled first within their category.

## AI Agenda Blurbs

For each agenda item, the system can generate a short two-to-three sentence summary suitable for inclusion in the agenda document. Click **Generate Blurb** on any item row; the AI reads the submission's content and draft within about 30 seconds. A pulsing indicator shows while the task is running.

Read every blurb before the agenda is finalised. The AI summarises factually but cannot exercise the kind of editorial judgment a Secretary brings — it may omit the politically sensitive aspect of a case or use language that requires softening. Treat it as a first draft, not a final product. Edit the blurb field directly; your edits are saved automatically.

::: {.callout-warning}
Do not distribute an agenda that contains unreviewed AI blurbs. Each item is marked *AI draft — verify* until you have edited the field, at which point the warning is removed.
:::

## The Briefing Pack

Once the agenda is finalised, generate the Commission briefing pack. This is the document each Commissioner receives before the sitting. From the meeting view, click **Generate Briefing Pack**.

The system compiles:
- The formal agenda in category order
- Each agenda item's blurb and key metadata
- Any documents flagged as Commission-facing by the processing unit

Review the generated pack carefully. Pay particular attention to whether any sensitive personal information that should be redacted from the Commissioners' copy is present. Download the pack, apply any necessary manual edits, and distribute through your established channel (email, shared drive, or printed copies as appropriate for your sitting format).

## The Day Before

<ul class="checklist">
<li>Agenda is finalised — no new items to be added after this point</li>
<li>All agenda items are in *Forwarded to Commission* or *Commission Sitting* stage</li>
<li>Blurbs reviewed and edited for all items</li>
<li>Briefing pack generated and distributed to all Commissioners</li>
<li>Quorum confirmed — sufficient Commissioner attendance</li>
<li>Meeting room set up (or virtual platform tested)</li>
<li>Any outstanding matters arising briefed to the Chairperson</li>
</ul>

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Section 3</span><span class="section-title">Running the Meeting</span></div>
```

# The Meeting Room

SCDMS provides a dedicated hub for managing the sitting in real time. Access it from **Commission Decision → Meeting Room**. This page is designed to be used on a laptop or secondary screen during the actual sitting.

## What the Meeting Room Shows

The left panel lists all agenda items grouped by category, with the current item highlighted. Clicking an item brings up the submission summary and decision controls in the main panel. The right column shows the stage history and notes field for recording discussion.

At the top of the page, a status bar shows:
- Current meeting date and reference
- Items resolved vs. total items
- Quorum status (green if met, red if attendance drops below quorum)

## Recording Decisions

For each item the Commission resolves, record the decision before moving to the next item. Decisions recorded in real time are more accurate than decisions recorded from memory after the sitting.

<ol class="procedure">
<li>Click the agenda item in the left panel. The item detail opens in the main panel.</li>
<li>Click **Record Decision**. A form slides in below the item detail.</li>
<li>Select the **Decision Type**: Approved, Approved with Conditions, Rejected, Deferred, Tabled, or Withdrawn.</li>
<li>Enter the **Decision Text** — the formal wording of the Commission's resolution. This text will appear in the official decision record and in the formal notification letter to the ministry. Write it as the Commission would sign off on it.</li>
<li>If conditions were attached, enter them in the **Conditions** field. Each condition should be a discrete, actionable statement.</li>
<li>Set the **Implementation Deadline** if the Commission specified one. This date triggers automatic reminders to the ministry.</li>
<li>Click **Save Decision**. The item is marked resolved in the agenda panel. The stage advances to *Decision* status.</li>
</ol>

If a decision is deferred to the next sitting without substantive discussion, use **Defer** and set the next meeting date. If the Commission makes a decision after extensive discussion that requires careful wording, use the **Draft** option to save a provisional text, then refine it before finalising.

::: {.callout-important}
The decision text you save is the authoritative record. It will be reproduced verbatim in formal correspondence and the decision register. If there is any ambiguity about what the Commission decided, clarify with the Chairperson before saving — do not guess.
:::

## Matters Arising

When the Commission revisits a matter from a previous sitting, it appears as a *Matter Arising* in the agenda. The item shows the originating decision for reference. Record the outcome of the revisit the same way as a fresh decision — use the **Record Decision** form and note in the decision text that this resolves (or continues) the matter arising from the earlier sitting reference.

## If Quorum Is Lost

If Commissioners leave and attendance falls below quorum during the sitting, the meeting room displays a quorum alert. At that point, no further decisions can be validly made. Note the time and the items resolved to that point. Remaining items are typically placed on the agenda for the next scheduled sitting. Record this in the meeting notes field.

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Section 4</span><span class="section-title">Post-Meeting Administration</span></div>
```

# Minutes, Decisions, and Notifications

The period immediately after a sitting is the busiest administrative phase. The sequence below reflects the correct order of operations — do not issue notifications before minutes are approved, and do not close the meeting record before notifications are confirmed sent.

## Drafting the Minutes

Navigate to the meeting and click **Edit Minutes**. The minutes editor opens a structured document with sections for:

- Opening (date, location, Commissioners present, staff in attendance, apologies)
- Confirmation of previous minutes
- Items resolved (in agenda order within each category)
- Items deferred or tabled
- General business
- Closure (time meeting closed, next meeting date)

The system pre-populates the items resolved section from the decisions recorded during the sitting. You will need to add the deliberation narrative for each item — a factual summary of the discussion, not a verbatim transcript.

**AI-assisted minutes:** If the sitting was recorded, the minutes extraction pipeline can transcribe and structure the audio. Navigate to the meeting's **Minutes Pipeline** tab and upload the recording. The AI produces a structured draft that you then edit for accuracy and language. This is a significant time saving for long sittings, but the draft requires careful review — the AI transcribes accurately but cannot distinguish between a Commissioner thinking aloud and making a formal statement.

## Circulating the Draft

Once the initial draft is complete, change the minutes status from *Draft* to *In Review* using the button in the minutes editor. This makes the document visible to Commissioners for review. Note the review deadline in the minutes header.

Collect amendments from Commissioners and incorporate them. When the minutes are agreed, change the status to *Approved*. The Chairperson's approval is required before minutes are finalised.

## Issuing Decision Notifications

Only after minutes are approved do you issue the formal decision notifications to ministries. Navigate to **Commission Decision → Notifications**. Each unissued decision appears as a pending notification.

For each notification:

<ol class="procedure">
<li>Open the notification record. The system has pre-populated the letter with the decision text, PSC reference number, ministry details, conditions (if any), and implementation deadline.</li>
<li>Review the salutation and address — confirm the correct ministry and the correct officer (typically the Permanent Secretary or the HR Manager who submitted the case).</li>
<li>Review the body text. The system uses a standard template, but you may need to adjust language for cases involving sensitive outcomes (rejection letters, in particular, should be written with care).</li>
<li>Attach any relevant documents — for instance, the approved extract of minutes for that item.</li>
<li>Click **Issue Notification**. The system sends the email and marks the notification as issued. The submission stage advances to *Decision Issued*.</li>
</ol>

::: {.callout-warning}
For **Rejection** and **Deferred Back to HR** decisions, consider calling the ministry HR Manager before the formal letter arrives. An unexpected rejection received without context creates unnecessary concern. A brief advance call is good practice and reflects well on the Commission.
:::

## Closing the Meeting Record

Once all notifications have been issued and the minutes are approved, close the meeting record. Go to the meeting detail page and click **Close Meeting**. This:

- Marks the meeting as completed in the meetings list
- Locks the agenda and decisions against further changes (except through a formal amendment process)
- Triggers any remaining follow-up task generation

Closed meeting records remain fully readable. The lock prevents accidental changes; it does not hide the record.

## The Decision Register

Navigate to **Commission Decision → Decisions** to access the formal decision register. This is the authoritative, searchable record of every Commission decision across all sittings.

Each entry shows the PSC reference number, officer name, form type, decision type, decision date, and whether implementation has been confirmed. The register can be searched by any field and exported to Excel or PDF for external reporting.

The register updates automatically as decisions are issued — there is no separate step to add entries to it.

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Section 5</span><span class="section-title">Task Management</span></div>
```

# Post-Decision Tasks

## Where Tasks Come From

Commission sittings often generate obligations beyond issuing decision letters — follow-up correspondence, requests for further information, regulatory notifications, or coordination with other agencies. These are recorded as tasks in SCDMS.

Tasks can be created in two ways:

- **From the minutes:** The minutes editor has a *Matters Arising* section where you can mark text as an action item. These become tasks automatically when you save.
- **Manually:** Navigate to **Commission Decision → Tasks** and click **New Task**.

## Managing the Task List

The task list shows all open tasks grouped by status: *To Do*, *In Progress*, and *Done*. Each task has:

- A title and description
- An assigned officer
- A due date
- A linked submission or meeting (where applicable)

As Secretary, you have visibility over all tasks. Filter the view to see only tasks assigned to you, or switch to *All Tasks* to monitor your team's workload.

When a task is completed, the assigned officer marks it done. You receive a notification confirming closure. If a task is overdue, it appears in red in the task list and on the dashboard. Follow up with the assigned officer directly; the system does not escalate overdue tasks automatically.

## Implementation Follow-Up

Implementation of Commission decisions is the ministry's responsibility, but monitoring it is yours. The **Awaiting Implementation** view on the dashboard lists all submissions in *Decision Issued* or *Under Implementation* status, sorted by deadline.

When a ministry uploads their implementation evidence and marks the submission as implemented, you receive a notification. Review the evidence:

- Is it appropriate for the type of decision? An appointment decision should be evidenced by a signed appointment letter; a discipline decision by confirmation of the action taken.
- Has the implementation deadline been met?
- Are any conditions from the decision addressed?

If satisfactory, no further action is needed — the submission closes automatically. If the evidence is insufficient, contact the ministry and request the correct documentation. Record your communications in the submission's notes field.

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Section 6</span><span class="section-title">Reports and Records</span></div>
```

# Reporting

Navigate to **Reports** in the left sidebar to access the full reporting module. The reports most relevant to the Secretary's work are:

**Commission Sitting Summary** — for a selected meeting, shows items resolved, items deferred, total time per item (where recorded), and Commissioner attendance. Useful for preparing the Chairperson's post-sitting report.

**Decision Register Export** — the full decision register for a selected period, exportable as Excel or a formatted PDF with the PSC letterhead. The standard quarterly report for Cabinet and the Minister is generated from this export.

**Submission Pipeline Report** — shows the current distribution of submissions across stages and units. Useful for identifying bottlenecks — if a large number of submissions are sitting in *Under Assessment* for an extended period, this becomes visible here.

**Ministry Compliance Report** — tracks implementation deadlines across all ministries. Ministries with consistently missed implementation deadlines should receive a formal reminder letter; this report provides the data to support that.

Reports can be scheduled to run automatically and delivered to a specified email address. To set up a scheduled report, click the **Schedule** button on any report page and configure the frequency and recipient.

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Section 7</span><span class="section-title">Tools</span></div>
```

# Staff Assistant

The Staff Assistant is accessible from the left navigation menu. It is a conversational AI tool trained on PSC procedures and connected to your active submission data.

Practical uses for the Secretary:

- **Drafting correspondence** — *"Draft a formal letter to the Ministry of Finance acknowledging receipt of submission PSC-2025-00341 and requesting the missing medical certificate."* The Assistant produces a draft in formal English that you then review and adapt.
- **Decision text phrasing** — *"Suggest formal wording for an approved-with-conditions decision for a Grade 12 appointment where the condition is completion of the Induction Programme within six months."*
- **Procedural queries** — *"What is the correct process for withdrawing a submission that has already been registered?"*
- **Summarising a submission** — *"Summarise submission PSC-2025-00215 for the Chairperson's briefing."*

The Assistant does not issue notifications, change submission stages, or modify official records. Its output is always a draft or an answer — you take the action.

# Keyboard Navigation

SCDMS supports keyboard shortcuts for common operations. A full list is available by pressing `?` at any time, or by clicking the keyboard icon in the page header.

The most useful shortcuts for daily Secretary work:

| Keys | Action |
|---|---|
| `/` | Open search |
| `g m` | Go to Meeting Room |
| `g t` | Go to Tasks |
| `g n` | Go to Notifications |
| `n` | New submission (rarely needed but available) |

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Appendix A</span><span class="section-title">Stage Transition Authority</span></div>
```

# Stage Transition Reference

The table below shows which role can perform each stage transition. The Secretary holds the broadest transition authority in the system.

| From stage | To stage | Who can act |
|---|---|---|
| Submitted to PSC | Received by PSC | Secretary, Senior Admin Officer |
| Received by PSC | Registered and Routed | Secretary, Senior Admin Officer |
| Registered and Routed | Manager Checklist Review | Secretary, Unit Manager |
| Manager Checklist Review | Under Assessment | Unit Manager, Secretary |
| Under Assessment | Forwarded to Commission | Secretary, Unit Manager |
| Any active stage | Returned for Clarification | Secretary, Unit Manager |
| Any active stage | On Hold (deferred/tabled) | Secretary |
| Forwarded to Commission | Commission Sitting | Secretary |
| Commission Sitting | Decision | Secretary |
| Decision | Decision Issued | Secretary |
| Decision Issued | Under Implementation | Ministry HR (automatic) |
| Under Implementation | Implementation Report | Ministry HR |

*Note: PSC Administrator has full transition authority. Unit Managers can act on stages within their unit's scope.*

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Appendix B</span><span class="section-title">Pre-Sitting Checklist</span></div>
```

# Pre-Sitting Checklist

Complete this checklist no later than the morning of the day before the sitting.

**Register**

<ul class="checklist">
<li>All items for this sitting are in <em>Forwarded to Commission</em> or later stage</li>
<li>No items still showing <em>Under Assessment</em> that were expected for this sitting</li>
<li>Duplicate submissions identified and resolved</li>
</ul>

**Agenda**

<ul class="checklist">
<li>All agenda items added and sequenced correctly</li>
<li>Categories confirmed with Chairperson</li>
<li>Matters arising from previous sittings linked correctly</li>
<li>All AI blurbs reviewed and edited</li>
<li>Agenda formally finalised (no further additions expected)</li>
</ul>

**Briefing pack**

<ul class="checklist">
<li>Briefing pack generated from current agenda state</li>
<li>Pack reviewed — sensitive information redacted where required</li>
<li>Pack distributed to all Commissioners</li>
<li>Chairperson has confirmed receipt and has no questions</li>
</ul>

**Logistics**

<ul class="checklist">
<li>Quorum confirmed in writing from each Commissioner</li>
<li>Apologies noted for Commissioners who cannot attend</li>
<li>Meeting room booked / virtual platform tested</li>
<li>Recording equipment ready (if minutes pipeline will be used)</li>
<li>Previous meeting's minutes on agenda for confirmation</li>
</ul>

---

```{=html}
<div class="section-break"><span class="section-num">Appendix C</span><span class="section-title">Glossary</span></div>
```

# Glossary

**Agenda blurb** — A brief summary of a submission prepared for inclusion in the Commission agenda document. May be drafted by AI and must be reviewed by the Secretary before distribution.

**Briefing pack** — The document set distributed to Commissioners before a sitting, containing the agenda and supporting summaries for each item.

**Decision register** — The complete, searchable record of all Commission decisions. Maintained automatically in SCDMS and available at *Commission Decision → Decisions*.

**Deferred** — A decision outcome where the Commission postpones consideration of a matter. The submission remains open and is typically placed on the next agenda.

**Implementation deadline** — The date set in the decision record by which the ministry must implement the decision and report back. Triggers automatic reminders.

**Matters arising** — Items carried over from a previous sitting for further consideration. Distinguished from new items by their formal link to the originating meeting reference.

**Minutes pipeline** — The AI-assisted process for extracting and structuring meeting minutes from an audio recording of the sitting.

**PSC reference number** — The formal identifier assigned during registration, in the format *PSC-YYYY-NNNNN*. All correspondence related to a submission uses this reference.

**Quorum** — The minimum number of Commissioners whose attendance is required for the Commission's decisions to be valid.

**Routing** — The assignment of a registered submission to a processing unit and, optionally, a specific officer within that unit.

**Stage** — One of the named states in the submission workflow. Each stage transition is recorded with a timestamp and the name of the officer who performed it.

**Tabled** — A submission placed on the agenda but not resolved at that sitting, typically for procedural reasons. It remains on the agenda for the next sitting without re-submission by the ministry.

**Withdrawal** — An irreversible action that closes a submission without a formal decision being made. Requires written authorisation from the submitting ministry.

---

*Office of the Public Service Commission — Vanuatu*
*For technical assistance contact your SCDMS administrator.*
